title: Standardized Pupal Irradiation and Emergence of Male Aedes aegypti for Sterile Insect Technique (SIT)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"8vmjf","text":"This protocol, developed by the Applied Science and Technologies Department of the Lee County Mosquito Control District, supports the mass rearing of Aedes aegypti for implementation in a Sterile Insect Technique (SIT) program. It details the standardized workflow for preparing, drying, irradiating, and managing the emergence of male pupae. Following sex-based sorting, pupae are partially dehydrated and weighed into target aliquots before undergoing irradiation with dosimetry controls to ensure dose accuracy. Post-irradiation, pupae are transferred to preconditioned release containers and held under controlled environmental conditions to facilitate emergence. Emerged adults are then drained and maintained with sucrose feeding until release. This protocol ensures consistent pupal handling, precise irradiation, and high adult survival—critical for the success of SIT-based mosquito control strategies.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":150,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"7nico","text":"Maintain pupal integrity – Handle pupae gently at all stages to avoid physical damage and stress.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":24}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6bvmp","text":"Avoid overdrying – In the dehydrator, aim for active, free-moving pupae without clumping; excessive drying can reduce survival.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"50v5u","text":"Work quickly during weighing – Pupae lose water rapidly; delays can affect weight accuracy and aliquot sizes.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":28}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3852e","text":"Check female contamination – Ensure female proportion is below threshold before irradiation to maintain release quality.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":26}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"all01","text":"Irradiator calibration – Regularly check dosimetry equipment and irradiation dose to ensure consistent sterilization levels.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2qq45","text":"Environmental control – Keep emergence containers at 26.7 °C (±3 °C) and ~80% RH for optimal emergence rates.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3865r","text":"Sugar feeding – Ensure cotton strips remain moist with 10% sucrose but not dripping to avoid excess humidity in containers.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"18p7m","text":"Post-draining moisture check – No standing water should remain before final holding to reduce mortality.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":28}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"oc6o","text":"Avoid overdrying pupae in dehydrator.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3p3bs","text":"Work quickly during weighing to minimize weight changes from water loss.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4qed0","text":"Do not overstress pupae when handling.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9kdt1","text":"Safety: Follow institutional biosafety and irradiation safety guidelines.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Using a fan makeup brush, gently disperse any clumps of pupae that may have formed. This ensures a uniform layer across the surface, which is essential for consistent drying.
Place coffee filters with pupae in the lowest level of the food dehydrator the lowest level the level above may be used.

Do not place pupae in the level immediately below the dehydrator fan and heating element. Do not over stress pupae. The drying procedures can be done on smaller scales to ensure that pupae do not dry out too much.

Once the coffee filters loaded with semi-moist pupae have been placed into the dehydrator trays, place dehydrator fan on top of the stacked trays and set dehydrator to lowest setting (in our case, this is 95°F or 35°C). Turn the dehydrator on for seven minutes.
While pupae dry, prepare a number of large weigh boats equivalent to the number of pupae-containing coffee filters.

Gently shake semi-dried pupae into coffee a filter. Repeat this step for all drying pupal piles (Figures 1 and 2). Figure 1: Piles of pupae have started to dry and disperse. They are loaded onto coffee filters. Figure 2: Dried pupae in coffee filters arranged on a tray in the dehydrator.
Collect the sorted male pupae and carefully remove any remaining larvae or pre-pupae to ensure consistency of the batch.
Ensure female contamination is below the acceptable threshold of 0.4%.
Pour pupae through a hand sieve, collecting the wastewater in an empty paint bucket. Dab hand sieve on absorbent microfiber cloth to remove excess water.

After seven minutes, turn off the dehydrator and inspect the pupae. They should appear highly active, with no large clumps of moisture remaining. If moist or clumped pupae are present, gently separate them using a fan makeup brush and return them to the dehydrator for an additional three minutes. Repeat this process as needed. Pupae are considered fully dried when they are no longer clumped and can move freely—this is often accompanied by a noticeable change in sound between wet and dry pupae.

nce it has been verified that they are dry, pour the contents of each coffee filter into a weigh boat (Figure 3). Line up the weigh boats near the balance. It is sometimes helpful, but not necessary, to have twopeople do this to speed the process along. One person will record the data while the other will weigh the pupae. Figure 3: Properly dried pupae in weigh boats. There should be no clumping at this stage.

Spread paper towels on counter. Quickly invert and firmly tap the hand sieve on the paper towel, leaving pupal contents in a neat pile. Continue these steps for the remaining pupae that need to be prepared forirradiation. During this time pupae will spread out slightly from the main mass.
Flip the pupal pile over so that addition water can be absorbed. The pupae are ready for the next step once the pupae are no longer in a single defined pile and many are able to move on their own.

Once the weighing has been completed, use the individual pupal weight to determine the weight of 2200 male pupae. Once this is known, obtain the screened irradiation trays. Then, as before, add a weigh boat to the scale, tare, and add pupae to reach a number equivalent to the weight of 2200 pupae. Add these weighed pupae to an irradiation tray and use a fan makeup brush to very gently disperse them throughout the area of the tray. Continue to weigh and aliquot pupae into the trays until all pupae have been dispensed.

The irradiation trays are stacked six high and placed into the holding chambers of the irradiator (Figure 4). With our current setup, we can irradiate 36 trays of 2200 pupae. If the trays cannot be filled entirely with pupae, use pearled couscous to fill in the remaining empty tray spaces. Prepare a glassine envelope with a dosimeter tag and film. Use a wax pencil to mark the date and time of irradiation. Place this glassine envelope in the center of the fourth irradiation tray from any stack of six trays. Once this has been placed and all trays are either filled with pupae or couscous, then close the irradiator door and proceed with irradiation Figure 4: Empty irradiation tray (A); stack of trays filled with pupae (note that there are six trays filled with pupae and an empty tray at the bottom to prevent pupae above from being damaged) (B); loading the irradiator canisters with trays (C).

Once irradiation has completed, remove the glassine envelope with dosimetry film and store for 24 hours before reading. The pupal trays are removed from the irradiator and the pupae can be added to release containers.

Screened lids are prepared by modifying the original paint bucket lids. Use a circular cutter to cut a large hole in the center of each lid. Then, glue a circular piece of screen material over the hole using a hot glue gun, ensuring the screen is securely attached and fully covers the opening. Inspect each lid carefully to confirm there are no tears, gaps, or other openings that could compromise containment or ventilation. Regular checks should be performed throughout the experiment to maintain lid integrity.

After irradiation, the pupae are transferred into release containers. These containers are 2.5-quart paint buckets with roughened interior surfaces and modified screen lids to allow for proper ventilation
To roughen the interior of the buckets, use a wire wheel attached to a drill. Scour the inner surface starting from the top lip down to approximately 1 cm above the bottom.
Cut a 4 cm slit ~1 cm above the bucket bottom for water drainage. This slit is used to drain the water from the bucket after the adult mosquitoes have emerged
Label each release container in accordance with the number of irradiation trays
Fill containers with filtered tap water up to the slit/water line while pupae are being irradiated.
Pour the contents of one irradiation tray into the water-filled release container, gently tapping the tray to dislodge any pupae clinging to the mesh, then secure the lid.

Lay a cotton strip across the lid; saturate with 10% sucrose solution (wet but not dripping). Sugar Preparation and Feeding by Rachel Morreale Figure 5: Release bucket with sterile mosquitoes. Note the sugar strip and the scuffed sides (A). At right, looking within a release bucket (B).
Keep the buckets in room at 26.7 ℃ (±3 ℃), RH ≅80% to allow emergence for 48 hours.

Once all containers have been drained, begin placing them upright—starting with the first container that was drained. Ensure there is no pooled water at the bottom of any container. If water remains, readjust or replace the wick and keep the container on its side until fully drained. Then, remove the paper towel wick, replace the sugar-soaked cotton strip on the lid, and store the containers at room temperature until release day. Figure 7: Once drained, the containers are set upright and sugars are replenished.
Once full emergence has been achieved, drain the buckets by pour water and remaining pupae into an 8 oz deli cup; place lid; label with container number for later dead pupae counts.
Insert a 3 × 12 cm paper towel wick into the drainage slit.

Place container on its side (slit down) on an absorbent towel to wick remaining water (see Fig. 6). Figure 6: Draining release containers (A). Drained containers are kept on their sides with a paper towel wick inserted to help remove any remaining water (B).
creator: Rachel Morreale
